title: Research Design
author: Vaz, Sara
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
The research design began with an exploration of potential topics through brainstorming, which led to defining the central research question and conducting a comprehensive literature review. This process aimed to identify gaps in existing studies and establish a theoretical foundation for the project. According to Kawamura (2020) and Hempel (2020), these steps enable the researcher to refine focus, build knowledge, and contribute meaningfully to the academic community. 
 The study adopted a mixed-methods approach, combining artistic practice, experiential learning, and qualitative research. As Creswell (2018) highlights, integrating qualitative and quantitative data deepens understanding and provides richer insights. Artistic practice served both as a research method and as a means of knowledge production, where experimentation, participation, and aesthetic experience play central roles, aligning with Munari’s (2008) notion of design methodology based on practice and experience. 
 This methodology allowed the researcher to collect both subjective and measurable data, using performance as a medium of inquiry. Audience feedback, gathered through Google Forms with open and closed questions, offered valuable perspectives for analysis and refinement. Ultimately, the combination of artistic practice, experience, and qualitative analysis provided a comprehensive and reflective framework for understanding and developing performative fashion-art experiments.

The research design began with an exploration of potential topics through brainstorming, which led to defining the central research question and conducting a comprehensive literature review. This process aimed to identify gaps in existing studies and establish a theoretical foundation for the project. According to Kawamura (2020) and Hempel (2020), these steps enable the researcher to refine focus, build knowledge, and contribute meaningfully to the academic community. 
 The study adopted a mixed-methods approach, combining artistic practice, experiential learning, and qualitative research. As Creswell (2018) highlights, integrating qualitative and quantitative data deepens understanding and provides richer insights. Artistic practice served both as a research method and as a means of knowledge production, where experimentation, participation, and aesthetic experience play central roles, aligning with Munari’s (2008) notion of design methodology based on practice and experience. 
 This methodology allowed the researcher to collect both subjective and measurable data, using performance as a medium of inquiry. Audience feedback, gathered through Google Forms with open and closed questions, offered valuable perspectives for analysis and refinement. Ultimately, the combination of artistic practice, experience, and qualitative analysis provided a comprehensive and reflective framework for understanding and developing performative fashion-art experiments.
